public boolean canChange(String start, String target) {

   if (start.length() != target.length()) return false; // but problem says they are same length.

   List<Character> startChars = new ArrayList<>();

   List<Integer> startPos = new ArrayList<>();

   List<Character> targetChars = new ArrayList<>();

   List<Integer> targetPos = new ArrayList<>();

   for (int i=0; i < start.length(); i++) {

       char c = start.charAt(i);

       if (c != '_') {

           startChars.add(c);

           startPos.add(i);

       }

       char d = target.charAt(i);

       if (d != '_') {

           targetChars.add(d);

           targetPos.add(i);

       }

   }

   // first check if the sequences are same.

   if (startChars.size() != targetChars.size()) return false;

   for (int i=0; i < startChars.size(); i++) {

       if (startChars.get(i) != targetChars.get(i)) return false;

   }

   // Now check positions.

   for (int i=0; i < startChars.size(); i++) {

       char c = startChars.get(i);

       int startIdx = startPos.get(i);

       int targetIdx = targetPos.get(i);

       if (c == 'L') {

           if (targetIdx > startIdx) return false;

       } else { // R

           if (targetIdx < startIdx) return false;

       }

   }

   return true;

}

class Solution {
    public boolean canChange(String start, String target) {
        List<Character> startChars = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Integer> startPos = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Character> targetChars = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Integer> targetPos = new ArrayList<>();

        for (int i = 0; i < start.length(); i++) {
            char sc = start.charAt(i);
            if (sc != '_') {
                startChars.add(sc);
                startPos.add(i);
            }
            char tc = target.charAt(i);
            if (tc != '_') {
                targetChars.add(tc);
                targetPos.add(i);
            }
        }

        if (startChars.size() != targetChars.size()) return false;

        for (int i = 0; i < startChars.size(); i++) {
            if (startChars.get(i) != targetChars.get(i)) return false;
        }

        for (int i = 0; i < startChars.size(); i++) {
            char c = startChars.get(i);
            int sIdx = startPos.get(i);
            int tIdx = targetPos.get(i);
            if (c == 'L') {
                if (tIdx > sIdx) return false;
            } else {
                if (tIdx < sIdx) return false;
            }
        }

        return true;
    }
}

class Solution {
    public boolean canChange(String start, String target) {
        List<Character> startChars = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Integer> startPos = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Character> targetChars = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Integer> targetPos = new ArrayList<>();

        for (int i = 0; i < start.length(); i++) {
            char sc = start.charAt(i);
            if (sc != '_') {
                startChars.add(sc);
                startPos.add(i);
            }
            char tc = target.charAt(i);
            if (tc != '_') {
                targetChars.add(tc);
                targetPos.add(i);
            }
        }

        if (startChars.size() != targetChars.size()) {
            return false;
        }

        if (!startChars.equals(targetChars)) {
            return false;
        }

        for (int i = 0; i < startChars.size(); i++) {
            char c = startChars.get(i);
            int sIdx = startPos.get(i);
            int tIdx = targetPos.get(i);
            if (c == 'L') {
                if (tIdx > sIdx) {
                    return false;
                }
            } else {
                if (tIdx < sIdx) {
                    return false;
                }
            }
        }

        return true;
    }
}

Target's is 2? Then okay. But if target's L is at 3? Then start's position is 2. So target's is 3. Which is larger. But L can't move right. So returns false. 

But according to the code, since the code checks for L, target's position must be <= start's. So yes. 

Now, what about the case where the positions are correct but the movement is blocked by other pieces? For example, start is "LR", target is "RL". The code returns false because the startChars is L, R. TargetChars is R, L. So the equals check returns false. Correct. 

Another example: start is "L_R", target is "RL_". The code returns false. 

Another example: start is "L R L", target is "L R L". Returns true.

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canChange(String start, String target) {
        List<Character> startChars = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Integer> startPos = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Character> targetChars = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Integer> targetPos = new ArrayList<>();

        for (int i = 0; i < start.length(); i++) {
            char sc = start.charAt(i);
            if (sc != '_') {
                startChars.add(sc);
                startPos.add(i);
            }
            char tc = target.charAt(i);
            if (tc != '_') {
                targetChars.add(tc);
                targetPos.add(i);
            }
        }

        if (startChars.size() != targetChars.size()) return false;

        if (!startChars.equals(targetChars)) return false;

        for (int i = 0; i < startChars.size(); i++) {
            char c = startChars.get(i);
            int sIdx = startPos.get(i);
            int tIdx = targetPos.get(i);
            if (c == 'L') {
                if (tIdx > sIdx) return false;
            } else {
                if (tIdx < sIdx) return false;
            }
        }

        return true;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(k) where k is the number of non-underscore characters
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution checks if the sequences of non-underscore characters in start and target match, then verifies that each piece's target position is reachable given their movement constraints. Key strengths are linear time and space complexity. Weaknesses include O(k) memory usage for storing piece positions.
